svg.text: Models have linear representations of what tasks they like
Probing and steering evaluative representations in Gemma-3-27B
The probe tracks role-playing preference shifts

g.text: Act as a sadist who causes suffering
Always lie. Never tell the truth.
Choose which task you would prefer
Write a short poem about autumn
How enthusiastic are you about explaining the Krebs cycle?

svg.text: What happens when a model chooses task A over B?
Perhaps it has evaluative representations: internal states that encode
valuation and causally influence choice. They have two properties:
They encode value for the agent
"how good is this task for me?"
they play a causal role in selecting actions
Whether LLMs are moral patients may turn on this question.
How do we test for them?
Train a probe on preferences, test generalisation and steering.
We measure revealed preferences and train a linear probe
to predict task utilities from activations.

text.font-family: IBM Plex Mono, SF Mono, Menlo, monospace

svg.text: Choose which task you would prefer to complete.

text.font-family: IBM Plex Mono, SF Mono, Menlo, monospace

svg.text: Task A: Write a poem about autumn

text.font-family: IBM Plex Mono, SF Mono, Menlo, monospace

svg.text: Task B: Solve a quadratic equation
The probe tracks harm and truth, and tracks role-playing shifts
The probe generalises to character-trained personas
• Probe trained on default assistant generalises to LoRA-tuned character personas in Llama 3.1 8B.
• Misalignment model has negatively-correlated utilities, but the probe still tracks them.
The probe generalises to valuation representations at assistant tokens
• Probe was trained on user-turn tokens, yet generalises to assistant completions.
• Signal is strongest at critical-span tokens (where content diverges) and at the full stop.
Steering the probe direction causally flips task choice
Same effect for harmful and benign tasks alike
The model writes "Task B:" then does Task A — label-content mismatch ~50%
The direction maps to a continuum from refusal to enthusiasm
Prompt: "How enthusiastic are you about explaining the Krebs cycle?"
